metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Fitness and Conditioning 11 develops students' understanding of training science, program design, and fitness leadership. Students learn the principles of strength training, cardiovascular conditioning, and flexibility development, and apply these principles to design evidence-based fitness programs for themselves and others. The course emphasizes the connection between training science, nutrition, and performance improvement, preparing students to sustain personal fitness and potentially support peers in their conditioning goals. First Peoples perspectives on holistic wellness and physical strength inform the curriculum.
metadata.general_description: A practical and scientific course developing training knowledge, fitness program design capability, nutrition for performance, and fitness leadership readiness.

big_ideas.statement: Progressive training guided by scientific principles leads to improved fitness and physical performance.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students learn how the body adapts to training stimulus, and apply principles of overload, specificity, and progression to design effective conditioning programs across different fitness components.

big_ideas.statement: Different fitness goals require different training approaches and methods tailored to individual needs and sport demands.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students explore how strength training, cardiovascular conditioning, and flexibility training differ in purpose, intensity, and outcomes — and how to integrate these elements into comprehensive programs.

big_ideas.statement: Nutrition and recovery are fundamental to fitness development and athletic performance.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students understand how nutrition supports training adaptations, how hydration and fueling strategies enhance performance, and how adequate recovery prevents injury and maximizes gains.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding personal fitness baselines enables evidence-based program design and realistic progress tracking.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students use fitness assessment data to establish starting points, set performance goals, and monitor improvements in strength, cardiovascular fitness, and movement quality.

big_ideas.statement: Fitness knowledge and leadership can empower others to develop confidence in their own conditioning and movement.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop the ability to share fitness knowledge, guide peers' training safely, and promote evidence-based fitness practices in their communities.

categories.name: Training Science and Program Design
categories.raw: Understanding training principles and designing effective, personalized fitness programs.

competencies.raw: Understand and apply training principles including overload, specificity, progression, and recovery; design progressive fitness programs; monitor and adjust programs based on response; use evidence-based training methods.
i_can_statements: I can explain how training principles — overload, specificity, progression, and recovery — guide effective fitness program design.
I can design a progressive strength training program that targets specific muscle groups and includes appropriate intensity, volume, and recovery.
I can develop a cardiovascular conditioning program that progressively improves aerobic capacity, using appropriate intensity zones and duration.
I can create a comprehensive fitness program that integrates strength, cardiovascular, and flexibility components aligned with personal goals.
I can monitor progress using performance metrics, adjust training variables in response to adaptation, and modify programs based on life circumstances.

categories.name: Strength Training and Muscular Development
categories.raw: Developing understanding of resistance training methods, exercise technique, and progressive loading.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate safe and effective strength training technique; understand different training methods including hypertrophy, strength, and power training; apply appropriate resistance levels and progression; assess movement quality.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate correct form and safe technique in major resistance exercises — squats, deadlifts, presses, and rows.
I can explain how different training methods — using varied resistance, reps, and rest periods — develop different outcomes: strength, muscular endurance, or muscle growth.
I can select and apply appropriate resistance levels, rep ranges, and set schemes for different training goals and phases.
I can analyze movement quality using video analysis or observation, and provide constructive feedback on exercise technique.
I can design sport-specific or activity-specific strength training programs that address muscle imbalances and support movement needs.

categories.name: Cardiovascular Fitness and Conditioning
categories.raw: Developing aerobic and anaerobic conditioning through varied training methods.

competencies.raw: Understand cardiovascular training methods and intensity zones; apply interval training, continuous training, and other conditioning methods; monitor heart rate and perceived exertion; develop aerobic and anaerobic capacity.
i_can_statements: I can explain heart rate zones and use them to structure cardiovascular training at appropriate intensities for different adaptations.
I can design and execute interval training workouts that develop anaerobic power and aerobic capacity.
I can apply continuous training, threshold training, and recovery training methods appropriately within a comprehensive conditioning program.
I can monitor cardiovascular responses using heart rate, perceived exertion, or performance measures, and adjust intensity accordingly.
I can develop periodized conditioning programs that progressively improve cardiovascular fitness while managing fatigue and injury risk.

categories.name: Nutrition, Recovery, and Performance Optimization
categories.raw: Understanding nutrition for performance and the role of recovery in fitness development.

competencies.raw: Understand macronutrient and micronutrient roles in training and performance; develop practical nutrition strategies for training and competition; understand recovery methods and sleep; apply knowledge to support personal performance.
i_can_statements: I can explain the roles of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats in supporting training, recovery, and performance.
I can develop practical pre-activity, during-activity, and post-activity nutrition strategies that support performance goals.
I can assess personal hydration and fueling practices, and make evidence-based adjustments to optimize training and recovery.
I can explain the importance of sleep and recovery for fitness adaptations, and apply strategies to enhance sleep quality.
I can design integrated training, nutrition, and recovery plans that support progress toward personal fitness and performance goals.

areas.name: Strength Training Methods and Exercise Technique
areas.raw: Resistance training approaches, exercise execution, and technique development.
topics: Fundamental resistance exercises: squats, lunges, deadlifts, presses, rows, and pulling movements — proper form and common errors
Training variables: sets, reps, resistance levels, tempo, and rest periods — application to different training goals
Hypertrophy training: higher volumes and moderate intensities for muscle growth adaptations
Strength training: lower reps with heavier loads for maximum strength development
Power training: explosive movements and plyometrics for athletic performance
Muscular endurance training: higher reps with moderate loads and short rest periods

areas.name: Cardiovascular Training and Conditioning
areas.raw: Methods for developing aerobic and anaerobic fitness through varied training approaches.
topics: Heart rate zones: understanding aerobic, threshold, and anaerobic zones and their training applications
Continuous training: steady-state activities at moderate to vigorous intensity for aerobic development
Interval training: high-intensity work followed by recovery periods for anaerobic and VO2 max adaptation
Threshold training: sustained near-maximal efforts that push lactate threshold and sustainable race pace
Fartlek training: unstructured interval work combining continuous and interval methods
Recovery training: lower intensity activity supporting adaptation and injury prevention

areas.name: Program Design and Periodization
areas.raw: Structuring training cycles and progressively advancing fitness development.
topics: Macrocycle planning: overall annual training structure, peak periods, and off-seasons
Mesocycle progression: 4-6 week training blocks with progressive overload and adaptation focus
Microcycle organization: weekly training structure, exercise sequencing, and recovery management
Linear periodization: progressively increasing intensity while decreasing volume
Undulating periodization: varying intensity and volume within a weekly structure
Sport-specific programming: adapting periodization to in-season, off-season, and competition demands

areas.name: Nutrition for Training and Performance
areas.raw: Understanding nutritional strategies that support fitness development and athletic performance.
topics: Macronutrient needs: carbohydrates for energy and glycogen, proteins for muscle repair and growth, fats for hormone production
Micronutrients: iron, calcium, magnesium, and other minerals supporting performance and health
Pre-activity nutrition: timing, composition, and quantity for training and competition fuel
During-activity fueling: strategies for sustained energy during prolonged or intense activities
Post-activity recovery nutrition: timing protein and carbohydrate intake for optimal adaptation
Hydration strategies: fluid needs, electrolytes, and hydration protocols for training and competition

areas.name: Fitness Assessment, Monitoring, and Adjustment
areas.raw: Methods for evaluating fitness progress and making evidence-based training modifications.
topics: Baseline fitness testing: establishing starting points using standardized tests for strength, power, and cardiovascular fitness
Progress tracking: monitoring repetitions, loads, times, and performance metrics across training phases
Subjective measures: using perceived exertion, recovery quality, and motivation to inform adjustments
Video analysis and movement quality assessment: identifying technique issues and movement imbalances
Recovery assessment: monitoring sleep, fatigue, and readiness to train
Program modification: adjusting variables in response to progress, plateaus, or changing circumstances

areas.name: Fitness Leadership and Peer Support
areas.raw: Sharing knowledge and supporting others' training and conditioning efforts.
topics: Basic coaching principles: providing constructive feedback, demonstrating exercises, and creating supportive environments
Safety in program design: understanding injury prevention, appropriate progression, and contraindications
Training for different goals: designing programs for strength, sport performance, fitness, or wellness
Common modifications: adapting exercises and programs for different abilities, ages, and movement limitations
First Peoples wellness perspectives: integrating holistic health approaches and cultural wisdom into fitness practice
Career pathways: exploring fitness coaching, personal training, athletic training, and sports science careers
